modules.explanation: Rational numbers are numbers that can be written as p/q, where q is not zero. They behave like a well-organised number family. Addition and multiplication are closed, commutative and associative for rational numbers. Zero is the additive identity and one is the multiplicative identity. Every rational number has a negative, and every non-zero rational number has a reciprocal.

questions.rationale: 3/5 is in the form p/q with q not zero.

questions.question_text: The additive identity for rational numbers is
questions.rationale: Adding zero does not change a rational number.
Multiplying by one leaves the number unchanged.
The reciprocal interchanges numerator and denominator.

questions.question_text: Which property is shown by a x (b + c) = a x b + a x c?
questions.rationale: Multiplication is distributed over addition.

modules.topic_name: Number Line and Rational Numbers Between Two Numbers
modules.explanation: Rational numbers can be placed on a number line. Positive numbers lie to the right of zero and negative numbers to the left. Between any two rational numbers, there are many more rational numbers. This is different from whole numbers, where there may be no whole number between two neighbours like 4 and 5.

questions.rationale: Negative rational numbers lie to the left of zero.
A positive rational number is greater than a negative one.

questions.question_text: Between two rational numbers, there are
questions.rationale: Rational numbers are dense on the number line.

questions.question_text: A rational number between 1/2 and 1 is
questions.rationale: 3/4 lies between 1/2 and 1.
The farther-left negative number is smaller.

modules.explanation: A linear equation in one variable has only one unknown, usually with power one. It is like a balance: whatever we do to one side, we must do to the other. We solve by simplifying expressions, collecting variable terms, and isolating the variable. Equations may have variables on one side or both sides.

questions.rationale: Subtract 5 from both sides.
Divide both sides by 3.
Move terms to get 2x = 8.

questions.question_text: A linear equation in one variable has variable power
questions.rationale: Linear equations use first power of the variable.

modules.explanation: Word problems become easier when we translate them into equations. First choose a variable for the unknown, then write the relationship given in the problem. Some equations contain brackets or fractions and must be reduced to simpler linear form before solving. This method turns everyday puzzles into clear mathematical steps.

questions.question_text: Twice a number is 18. The equation is
questions.rationale: Twice means multiplied by 2.
Multiply both sides by 3.
Multiply 3 by both terms inside brackets.

questions.question_text: If a number increased by 9 is 20, the number is
To solve equations, equality is kept by doing
questions.rationale: An equation works like a balance.

modules.explanation: A polygon is a closed figure made of line segments. Polygons can be classified by number of sides and by shape, such as convex or concave, regular or irregular. Diagonals join non-adjacent vertices. The angle sum of a polygon depends on its number of sides, and the exterior angles of any polygon add up to 360 degrees.

questions.rationale: A polygon is a closed figure made of line segments.
Diagonals connect vertices that are not next to each other.

questions.question_text: Sum of exterior angles of a polygon is
questions.rationale: The exterior angle sum is always 360 degrees.
Regular means all sides and angles are equal.
Quad means four.

modules.explanation: Quadrilaterals include trapeziums, kites, parallelograms, rhombuses, rectangles and squares. In a parallelogram, opposite sides are parallel and equal, opposite angles are equal, and diagonals bisect each other. A rectangle has right angles, a rhombus has equal sides, and a square combines both features.

questions.question_text: In a parallelogram, opposite sides are
questions.rationale: This is a key property of parallelograms.
Each diagonal cuts the other into two equal parts.

questions.question_text: A rectangle has all angles equal to
questions.rationale: A rectangle has four right angles.
A rhombus is a parallelogram with equal sides.
It has right angles and all sides equal.

modules.explanation: Practical geometry uses ruler, compass and protractor to construct exact figures. A quadrilateral can be constructed when enough measurements are known, such as four sides and a diagonal, three sides and two diagonals, two adjacent sides and three angles, or three sides and two included angles. Construction is like following a precise drawing recipe.

questions.question_text: A quadrilateral can be constructed if four sides and one ___ are given.
questions.rationale: Four sides and a diagonal give enough structure in the textbook construction.

questions.question_text: Which tools are commonly used in practical geometry?
questions.rationale: These tools support accurate construction.

questions.question_text: A diagonal of a quadrilateral joins
questions.rationale: A diagonal joins non-adjacent vertices.
Accurate construction depends on the provided data.

questions.question_text: A special case of quadrilateral construction includes
questions.rationale: The chapter includes special quadrilateral cases.

modules.topic_name: Grouped Data, Bar Graphs and Pie Charts
modules.explanation: Data handling helps us collect, organise and understand information. Large data can be grouped into classes to make patterns easier to see. Bar graphs compare categories, while histograms show grouped numerical data. A pie chart represents a whole as a circle divided into sectors, where each sector shows a part of the total.

questions.question_text: Grouped data is useful when data is
questions.rationale: Grouping makes large data easier to read.

questions.question_text: A pie chart represents data using
questions.rationale: A pie chart divides a circle into sectors.
Histograms show frequency for class intervals.

questions.question_text: In a pie chart, the whole circle represents
questions.rationale: The full circle stands for the complete data set.
Bars make category comparison clear.

modules.explanation: Probability measures chance. Some outcomes are certain, some impossible, and some equally likely. When outcomes are equally likely, probability of an event is the number of favourable outcomes divided by the total number of outcomes. Tossing a coin, rolling a die and drawing a card are simple probability situations.

questions.question_text: The probability of a certain event is
questions.rationale: A certain event always happens.

questions.question_text: The probability of an impossible event is
questions.rationale: An impossible event cannot happen.

questions.question_text: A coin has how many equally likely outcomes?
questions.rationale: Head and tail are the two outcomes.

questions.question_text: On a die, probability of getting 3 is
questions.rationale: There is one favourable outcome out of six.
Probability measures likelihood.

modules.explanation: A square number is made by multiplying a number by itself, like 5 x 5 = 25. Square numbers form patterns: 1, 4, 9, 16 and so on. The chapter also connects square numbers with Pythagorean triplets, where three numbers can form the sides of a right triangle. Patterns make squares easier to recognise.

questions.question_text: The square of an even number is
questions.rationale: Even times even gives even.

questions.question_text: A Pythagorean triplet is related to
questions.rationale: Triplets satisfy the Pythagoras relation.
They are n x n values.

modules.explanation: A square root reverses squaring. If 9 is 3 squared, then the square root of 9 is 3. Square roots can be found by repeated subtraction, prime factorisation, division method, and estimation. Decimal square roots also follow place value carefully. Square roots help in measurement and geometry.

questions.question_text: Square root is the reverse of
questions.rationale: It finds the number multiplied by itself.
Prime factors are grouped in pairs for square root.

questions.question_text: The square root of 100 lies exactly at

modules.explanation: A cube number is made by multiplying a number by itself three times, such as 4 x 4 x 4 = 64. A cube root reverses this process. In prime factorisation, a perfect cube has prime factors grouped in triples. Cubes help students understand volume and number patterns.

questions.question_text: In prime factorisation, perfect cubes form groups of
questions.rationale: Cube roots use triples of equal prime factors.

modules.explanation: Percent means out of 100. It helps compare increases, decreases, discounts, profit and loss. Discount reduces marked price. Profit happens when selling price is more than cost price, and loss happens when selling price is less. Percentages make shopping, marks and business calculations easier to compare.

questions.rationale: Discount reduces the marked price.

questions.question_text: If CP = Rs 100 and SP = Rs 120, profit is
questions.rationale: Profit = SP - CP.

questions.question_text: If SP is less than CP, there is
questions.rationale: Loss occurs when selling price is lower than cost price.

modules.topic_name: Sales Tax, GST and Compound Interest
modules.explanation: Taxes such as sales tax, VAT or GST are added to the price of an item. Compound interest is interest calculated on the amount from the previous period, not just the original principal. This means money grows faster than in simple interest. The formula helps calculate repeated yearly or half-yearly growth.

questions.question_text: GST is usually added to the
questions.rationale: GST is a tax on goods and services.

questions.question_text: In compound interest, interest is calculated on
questions.rationale: Each period uses the new amount.
Amount is total money after interest.

questions.question_text: If interest is compounded annually, it is added
questions.rationale: Annually means once per year.

questions.question_text: Compound interest is generally greater than simple interest for same rate and time because
questions.rationale: Interest is added to amount and then earns more interest.

modules.explanation: Algebraic expressions are made from variables, constants and operations. Terms are separated by plus or minus signs. Like terms have the same variables with the same powers, so they can be added or subtracted. Multiplication can involve monomials, binomials and polynomials. Careful grouping keeps algebra neat.

questions.question_text: In 5x, the coefficient of x is
questions.rationale: The numerical factor is the coefficient.
They have the same variable with same power.
Mono means one.
Bi means two.
Add coefficients of like terms.

modules.explanation: An identity is an equation true for all allowed values of its variables. Standard identities such as (a + b)^2, (a - b)^2 and (a + b)(a - b) make algebraic multiplication faster. They are like reliable shortcuts, but they must be applied carefully with the right terms.

questions.rationale: This is the standard square identity.
The middle term is negative.
This is the difference of squares identity.
Identities hold generally.
Identities are algebraic shortcuts.

modules.topic_name: Views, Maps, Faces, Edges and Vertices
modules.explanation: Solid shapes are three-dimensional, but we often draw or view them on flat surfaces. A 3-D object can have different front, side and top views. Maps represent space using symbols and directions. Solids also have faces, edges and vertices. Euler's relation connects these parts for many polyhedra.

questions.rationale: A cube has length, breadth and height.
Top view is seen from above.

questions.question_text: A face of a solid is
questions.rationale: Faces are surfaces of solids.
Edges are line segments of solids.
Vertices are corner points.

modules.topic_name: Area of Trapezium, Quadrilaterals and Polygons
modules.explanation: Mensuration measures area, surface area and volume. A trapezium has one pair of parallel sides, and its area uses the sum of parallel sides and height. General quadrilaterals and polygons can be split into triangles and familiar shapes. Breaking complex figures into simpler parts makes area calculation manageable.

options: 1/2 x sum of parallel sides x height
questions.correct_answer: 1/2 x sum of parallel sides x height
questions.rationale: This is the trapezium area formula.
This is the defining feature used in the chapter.

questions.question_text: Complex polygons can be split into
questions.rationale: Area is easier after decomposition.
Area covers surface.

questions.question_text: Height used in area formulas is usually
questions.rationale: Area formulas use perpendicular height.

modules.topic_name: Surface Area and Volume of Solids
modules.explanation: Surface area measures the total outer covering of a solid, like wrapping paper around a box. Volume measures the space inside a solid, like water a container can hold. Cubes, cuboids and cylinders have formulas for surface area and volume. Capacity is closely related to volume and is often measured in litres.

questions.rationale: Volume uses length, breadth and height.
Volume measures space inside a solid.
Surface area is total area of faces.
A cylinder has two circular bases.
Capacity describes how much a container can hold.

modules.explanation: Exponents are shortcuts for repeated multiplication. Class 8 extends this idea to negative exponents: a negative exponent shows a reciprocal power. Powers of 10 help write very large and very small numbers in standard form. This is useful in science, measurements and comparing quantities of very different sizes.

questions.rationale: A negative exponent gives reciprocal power.
a^-1 = 1/a for non-zero a.
Move decimal six places to make 7.

options: very large and very small numbers
questions.correct_answer: very large and very small numbers
questions.rationale: It expresses numbers compactly.

modules.explanation: Two quantities are in direct proportion when they increase or decrease together in the same ratio. If more notebooks cost more money at a fixed price, cost and number of notebooks are directly proportional. The ratio between corresponding values remains constant, making unitary method and tables useful.

questions.rationale: Direct proportion keeps the same ratio.

questions.question_text: If 1 pen costs Rs 5, 4 pens cost
questions.rationale: Cost increases directly with number of pens.
Corresponding ratios are equal.

questions.question_text: More distance at same speed needs
questions.rationale: Distance and time are direct at constant speed.

options: Number of items and total cost at fixed price
Workers and time for same work
Speed and time for same distance
questions.correct_answer: Number of items and total cost at fixed price
questions.rationale: More items cost more at a fixed rate.

modules.explanation: Two quantities are in inverse proportion when one increases as the other decreases, while the product remains constant. For the same work, more workers take less time. For the same distance, higher speed takes less time. Inverse proportion helps solve practical problems involving work, speed, time and sharing.

questions.question_text: In inverse proportion, if one quantity increases, the other
questions.rationale: They move in opposite directions.

questions.question_text: For the same work, more workers need
questions.rationale: Work is shared among more workers.

questions.question_text: In inverse proportion, the product is
questions.rationale: Corresponding products remain equal.

questions.question_text: For the same distance, higher speed means
questions.rationale: Speed and time are inverse for fixed distance.

options: Workers and time for same work
Items and cost at fixed price
questions.correct_answer: Workers and time for same work
questions.rationale: More workers reduce time for same work.

modules.explanation: Factorisation means writing an expression as a product of its factors. Just as 12 can be written as 3 x 4, algebraic expressions can also be broken into factors. Common factors, regrouping, identities and forms like (x + a)(x + b) help factorise expressions. Factorisation is the reverse of multiplication.

questions.rationale: Multiplication expands; factorisation breaks into factors.
3x divides both terms.
This uses the difference of squares identity.
Regrouping reveals common factors.

questions.question_text: Factors of algebraic expressions multiply to give the
questions.rationale: Factors are product parts.

modules.explanation: Division of algebraic expressions uses factors. A monomial can be divided by another monomial by dividing coefficients and subtracting powers of like variables. A polynomial divided by a monomial is handled term by term. Factorisation makes division simpler and also helps detect common errors.

questions.rationale: Coefficients divide and x cancels.
Subtract exponents for same base.
Divide each term by 3.

questions.question_text: Polynomial divided by monomial is divided
questions.rationale: Each term is divided by the monomial.
Common factors can be cancelled.

modules.explanation: Graphs show information visually. Bar graphs compare categories, pie graphs show parts of a whole, histograms show grouped data, and line graphs show change. Coordinates locate a point on a plane using an ordered pair (x, y). The x-coordinate tells horizontal movement and the y-coordinate tells vertical movement.

questions.question_text: A line graph is useful to show
questions.rationale: Line graphs show trends.
Coordinates identify position.
The first number is the x-coordinate.
The second number is the y-coordinate.

questions.question_text: A pie graph is also called a
questions.rationale: Pie graphs divide a circle into sectors.

modules.explanation: A linear graph is a straight-line graph. It can show relationships such as cost versus number of items or distance versus time at a constant speed. To draw a graph, make a table of values, plot points using coordinates, and join them appropriately. Graphs turn number patterns into visible relationships.

questions.question_text: A linear graph is usually a
questions.rationale: Linear relationships form straight-line graphs.

questions.question_text: To draw a graph, we first often make a
questions.rationale: Tables give points to plot.

questions.question_text: The horizontal axis is commonly called the
questions.rationale: The x-axis runs horizontally.

questions.question_text: The vertical axis is commonly called the
questions.rationale: The y-axis runs vertically.

questions.question_text: A graph of distance-time at constant speed is
questions.rationale: Constant speed gives a straight-line relationship.

modules.topic_name: Numbers in General Form and Digit Games
modules.explanation: Numbers can be written in general form using place value. A two-digit number with tens digit a and ones digit b is 10a + b. This helps explain digit games and patterns logically. Letters can stand for digits, but each letter must represent a valid digit and follow place-value rules.

questions.question_text: A two-digit number with tens digit a and ones digit b is
questions.rationale: The tens digit has value 10a.

questions.question_text: If a = 3 and b = 5, 10a + b equals

questions.rationale: The ones digit becomes tens digit.
Each letter stands for a digit in such puzzles.

questions.question_text: Place value is important because digit position changes
questions.rationale: A digit's value depends on its place.

modules.explanation: Divisibility tests are quick rules for checking whether one number divides another exactly. A number ending in 0 is divisible by 10. A number ending in 0 or 5 is divisible by 5. A number ending in an even digit is divisible by 2. For 3 and 9, we use the sum of digits.

questions.question_text: A number divisible by 10 ends in
questions.rationale: The divisibility test for 10 checks the ones digit.

questions.question_text: A number divisible by 5 ends in
questions.rationale: This is the divisibility rule for 5.

questions.question_text: A number divisible by 2 has ones digit
questions.rationale: Even endings are divisible by 2.
If digit sum is divisible by 9, the number is divisible by 9.

questions.question_text: For divisibility by 3, digit sum should be divisible by
questions.rationale: The rule for 3 uses digit sum.
